docs.title: BIO727P Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the unified project documentation.

docs.title: Homepage navigation
docs.text:
The homepage acts as the main entry point to the platform. Use it to move between the major parts of the application quickly:

  • Home keeps you on the public landing page.
  • Workspace opens the experiment workspace, where sequence processing, analysis, and report generation are managed.
  • User Guide opens the documentation hub so users can jump directly to parsing, metrics, visualisation, and bonus-guide sections.
  • The results preview carousel on the homepage gives a quick view of the kinds of plots and outputs the platform can generate before entering the workspace.

The homepage navigation bar is intended to provide lightweight orientation, while the experiment workspace handles the step-by-step pipeline workflow.

docs.title: Documentation Areas
docs.text:
• Parsing & QC: Data ingestion, validation rules, quality-control checks, and API usage.
  • Start here: Parsing & QC Overview
  • PostgreSQL & Visualization: Database design, pipelines, and generated plots.
  • Start here: PostgreSQL & Visualization Overview
  • Bonus Visualisations: Advanced optional analysis outputs such as activity landscapes, mutation fingerprinting, domain enrichment, and mutation frequency summaries.
  • Start here: Bonus Visualisations Overview

docs.title: Ownership Model
docs.text:
Each area keeps its own OWNERS.md file and scoped assets/content paths:

  • docs/parsing_qc/*
  • docs/postgresql_visualization/*

This keeps contribution boundaries explicit while publishing through one MkDocs site.

docs.title: Bonus Visualisations
docs.text: The bonus visualisation guide covers the optional Section 6 outputs generated after the main analysis completes. These views extend the core result visualisations with deeper exploratory plots for mutation patterns and advanced comparisons.

docs.title: Included views
docs.text:
• Activity Landscape: interactive embedded landscape view for mutation embeddings and activity patterns.
  • Mutation Fingerprinting: per-variant mutation profile across the protein sequence.
  • Domain Enrichment Heatmap: domain-level mutation enrichment summaries.
  • Mutation Frequency: position-based mutation hotspot overview.

docs.title: When to use this section
docs.text: Use the bonus guide when you want to understand the advanced Section 6 outputs shown in the experiment workspace, including what each plot means, what data it depends on, and how it should be interpreted.

docs.title: Suggested reading path
docs.text:
1. Activity Landscape
  2. Mutation Fingerprinting
  3. Domain Enrichment Heatmap
  4. Mutation Frequency
  5. Pipeline Usage

docs.title: Database Notes
docs.text:
The bonus visualisation pipeline reads from the same experiment data used by the core analysis layer. It depends on:

  • experiment and generation records
  • variant records
  • mutation rows
  • derived activity metrics when available

docs.title: Data dependencies
docs.text:
Some bonus plots can run with mutation data alone, while others also require derived activity_score values.

  • Activity-dependent:
  • Activity Landscape
  • Activity Surface
  • Mutation Trajectory
  • Mutation/domain-dependent:
  • Mutation Frequency
  • Domain Enrichment
  • Mutation Fingerprinting

If a required dataset is missing, the platform keeps the bonus view visible and shows an explanatory placeholder instead of silently removing the section.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: The bonus pipeline is normally triggered from the application, but the logic is still organised as a dedicated pipeline component.

docs.title: Key runtime expectations
docs.text:
• it runs after the main analysis step
  • it targets the latest generation of the current experiment
  • it writes HTML outputs for the bonus explorer in Section 6

docs.title: Output behavior
docs.text:
For stable workspace rendering, the platform uses predictable bonus output targets so the UI can always look for the same view slots.

When a bonus plot cannot be produced, the platform writes a placeholder page for that slot instead of removing the slot entirely.

docs.title: Pipeline Usage
docs.text: Bonus visualisations are generated as part of Step 5: Run Analysis in the workspace.

docs.title: Execution model
docs.text: The bonus pipeline runs after the main report outputs are refreshed. It uses the latest generation of the current experiment and attempts to generate as many bonus views as possible.

docs.title: Best-effort behavior
docs.text:
The pipeline is intentionally best-effort:

  • if one bonus view fails, the others can still be written
  • if a required dataset is missing, the corresponding view is replaced by a clear placeholder page

This keeps Section 6 usable even when a particular plot cannot be computed for the current experiment state.

docs.title: Relationship to the main analysis
docs.text:
• Step 4 computes sequence outputs and mutation data
  • Step 5 computes the main analysis outputs and then generates the bonus visualisations

Because of this, bonus outputs should be interpreted as analysis-stage outputs, not sequence-stage outputs.

docs.title: Activity Landscape
docs.text: The Activity Landscape is an interactive view that maps variants in a reduced embedding space and compares those positions against activity-related values.

docs.title: What it shows
docs.text:
• relative clustering of variants
  • how high- and low-performing variants distribute across the embedding
  • broad performance regions rather than only rank-order summaries

docs.title: Why it is useful
docs.text: This view helps users see whether strong variants form clear clusters or whether performance is scattered across many mutation patterns.

docs.title: When it may be unavailable
docs.text: This plot depends on valid activity-related metrics. If those are not available for the latest generation, the workspace shows a placeholder explaining why the plot was skipped.

docs.title: Domain Enrichment Heatmap
docs.text: The Domain Enrichment Heatmap summarizes how mutation burden is distributed across annotated protein regions.

docs.title: What it shows
docs.text:
• which domains accumulate more non-synonymous changes
  • how mutation density differs between protein regions
  • how domain-level mutation load changes across generations

docs.title: Why it is useful
docs.text: This view helps distinguish broad sequence drift from focused regional change, which is useful when interpreting whether evolution is concentrating on specific functional parts of the protein.

docs.title: Mutation Fingerprinting
docs.text: Mutation Fingerprinting focuses on one selected variant and displays the pattern of non-synonymous amino-acid changes along the protein sequence.

docs.title: What it shows
docs.text:
• the positions of amino-acid changes
  • the spread of changes along the protein
  • a signature-like overview of what makes the chosen variant distinct

docs.title: Why it is useful
docs.text: This view is helpful for quickly understanding whether a top variant is shaped by a small number of concentrated changes or by a broader distributed mutation pattern.

docs.title: Mutation Frequency
docs.text: The Mutation Frequency view highlights mutation hotspots across the protein sequence.

docs.title: What it shows
docs.text:
• which positions are mutated most often
  • how mutation concentration changes across positions
  • where repeated sequence pressure appears across the experiment

docs.title: Why it is useful
docs.text: This plot helps identify recurring hotspot positions and supports comparisons between broad mutation load and more specific localized hotspots.

docs.title: BIO727P Data Parsing & Quality Control
docs.text:
Welcome to the documentation for the Data Parsing & Quality Control Module of the BIO727P Directed Evolution Web Portal.

  • Upload Data

    Upload TSV, CSV, or JSON experimental data files with automatic parsing and validation.

    Getting Started

  • Quality Control

    Two-tier validation system with adaptive thresholds and critical safety limits.

    QC Overview

  • API Reference

    REST API endpoints for programmatic data upload and validation.

    API Docs

  • Troubleshooting

    Common issues and solutions for data upload problems.

    Get Help

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text: Feature Description Multi-format Support Parse TSV, CSV, and JSON files with automatic format detection Dynamic Schema Accept additional metadata columns without configuration changes Adaptive Thresholds Percentile-based warnings adjust to each dataset Critical Limits Fixed safety bounds catch impossible values Batch Processing Efficient upsert operations for large datasets

docs.title: Version
docs.text:
• Module Version: 1.0.0
  • Last Updated: February 2026

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: • docs/parsing_qc/**

docs.title: Ownership Responsibilities
docs.text:
• Keep parsing and quality-control behavior docs aligned with code changes.
  • Update API docs when parsing endpoints or payload contracts change.
  • Maintain troubleshooting guidance for upload/validation failures.

docs.title: Change Rules
docs.text:
• Any parsing or QC logic change should include corresponding docs updates in this scope.
  • Keep examples executable and file paths repository-relative.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This project uses a Flask application-factory pattern with blueprint-based routing and service-layer business logic.

docs.title: Folder Ownership Rules
docs.text:
• app/blueprints/*: HTTP routes and request/response orchestration only.
  • app/services/*: domain logic, external integrations, and data processing.
  • app/models.py: SQLAlchemy models.
  • app/templates/ and app/static/: UI assets.
  • scripts/: one-off or operational entry scripts; not imported by app runtime.
  • tests/: automated verification.

docs.title: Key Decisions
docs.text:
• Single app factory at app/__init__.py.
  • Single routing pattern via blueprints in app/blueprints/.
  • Single UniProt integration module at app/services/uniprot_service.py.
  • Analysis code source of truth in app/services/analysis/.

docs.title: Conventions
docs.text:
• New routes must be added through a blueprint package under app/blueprints/.
  • New business logic should be added under the appropriate app/services/<domain>/.
  • Avoid introducing parallel package roots (for example, a second src/ runtime tree).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Data Parsing & QC module enables researchers to upload, parse, and validate directed evolution experimental data. It provides robust file handling, automatic field normalisation, and a two-tier quality control system that combines dataset-adaptive thresholds with absolute safety limits.

docs.title: Accessing the Upload Page
docs.text:
Navigate to the upload page at: 

http://your-server:5000/parsing/upload

docs.title: Uploading Data
docs.text:
1. Select an Experiment: Choose an existing experiment from the dropdown, or enter a new experiment ID
  2. Choose File: Select your TSV, CSV, or JSON data file
  3. Submit: Click "Upload" to process your data

docs.title: TSV/CSV Files
docs.text:
Tab-separated (.tsv) or comma-separated (.csv) text files. The parser automatically detects the delimiter.

Expected Structure: 

Plasmid_Variant_Index   Parent_Plasmid_Variant  Directed_Evolution_Generation   Assembled_DNA_Sequence  DNA_Quantification_fg   Protein_Quantification_pg
1   -1  0   ATGCGATCG...    520.5   50.2
2   1   1   ATGCGATCG...    615.3   48.7

docs.title: JSON Files
docs.text:
JSON files containing an array of variant records or a nested object with a records or variants key.

Supported Structures: 

// Array of objects
[
  {"Plasmid_Variant_Index": 1, "Directed_Evolution_Generation": 0, ...},
  {"Plasmid_Variant_Index": 2, "Directed_Evolution_Generation": 1, ...}
]

// Nested with 'records' key
{
  "records": [
    {"Plasmid_Variant_Index": 1, ...}
  ]
}

docs.title: Required Fields
docs.text:
The following fields must be present in every record:

Field Name Description Accepted Aliases variant_index Unique identifier for the plasmid variant Plasmid_Variant_Index, variant_id, id generation Directed evolution generation number (0 = wild-type) Directed_Evolution_Generation, gen, round assembled_dna_sequence Full DNA sequence of the variant Assembled_DNA_Sequence, sequence, dna_sequence dna_yield DNA quantification measurement DNA_Quantification_fg, dna_concentration protein_yield Protein quantification measurement Protein_Quantification_pg, protein_concentration

docs.title: Optional Fields
docs.text: Field Name Description Accepted Aliases parent_variant_index Parent variant ID (-1 or empty for generation 0) Parent_Plasmid_Variant, parent_id control Whether this is a control sample Control, is_control

docs.title: Additional Metadata
docs.text: Any columns not listed above are automatically captured and stored as additional metadata. This allows flexibility to include experiment-specific fields without modifying the parser.

docs.title: Quality Control System
docs.text: The QC system uses a two-tier threshold architecture to identify potential data issues:

docs.title: Tier 1: Warnings (Adaptive Thresholds)
docs.text:
Warnings flag statistical outliers relative to the uploaded dataset. These use percentile-based thresholds computed dynamically.

How it works: 1. All yield values are collected from the uploaded dataset 2. The 1st percentile (P1) and 99th percentile (P99) are calculated 3. Values outside this range are flagged as warnings

Configuration: 

Percentile Low:  P1  (1st percentile)
Percentile High: P99 (99th percentile)
Minimum Samples: 30  (required for stable percentile calculation)

Example: If your dataset has DNA yields ranging from 400–1800 fg: - Values below ~405 fg (P1) → Warning: "Unusually low DNA yield" - Values above ~1750 fg (P99) → Warning: "Unusually high DNA yield"

docs.title: Tier 2: Errors (Critical Safety Limits)
docs.text:
Errors flag values that are biologically or instrumentally impossible. These are fixed limits based on assay specifications.

Metric Critical Minimum Critical Maximum DNA Yield 300 fg 5000 fg Protein Yield 20 pg 2000 pg

Values outside these limits generate errors and the record is rejected.

docs.title: Why This Approach?
docs.text: Approach Limitation Fixed thresholds only Don't account for batch-to-batch variation Z-scores Assume normal distribution (yield data often isn't) Percentiles + Critical Limits ✅ Non-parametric, robust to skewed distributions, catches impossible values

docs.title: Visual Representation
docs.text:
CRITICAL MIN        P1 WARNING       MEDIAN        P99 WARNING      CRITICAL MAX
             |                 |               |               |                |
    ═════════╪═════════════════╪═══════════════╪═══════════════╪════════════════╪═════════
       ERROR │    WARNING      │           OK  │    WARNING    │     ERROR
     (Reject)│   (Flag only)   │    (Accept)   │   (Flag only) │    (Reject)

docs.title: Per-Record Validation
docs.text:
Each record is individually checked for:

Check Type Description Required fields Error All required fields must be present and non-empty Data types Error variant_index, generation must be integers; yields must be numeric DNA sequence characters Error Only A, T, G, C, N allowed Sequence length Warning Flagged if unusually short (<100 bp) or long (>10,000 bp) Yield ranges Warning/Error Based on adaptive + critical thresholds Negative yields Warning Negative values flagged as potential measurement errors

docs.title: Cross-Record Validation
docs.text:
The entire dataset is validated for consistency:

Check Type Description Duplicate variant indices Error Each variant_index must be unique Missing generation 0 Warning Dataset should include wild-type control (generation=0) Generation continuity Warning Checks for gaps in generation numbers (e.g., 0,1,3 missing 2) Orphaned parents Warning Parent variant IDs that don't exist in the dataset

docs.title: Upload Results
docs.text: After processing, you'll see one of two outcomes:

docs.title: Successful Upload
docs.text:
✅ Upload Successful

Your data has been parsed, validated, and stored in the database.

Summary:
- Total Records: 301
- Inserted: 285
- Updated: 16
- Warnings: 3

⚠️ Warnings (3)
- Row 45: Unusually high DNA yield (1923.5) - may indicate measurement error
- Row 112: protein yield (18.2) below typical range for this batch
- Missing generation 4. Found generations [0,1,2,3,5,6]. This may indicate incomplete data.

docs.title: Failed Upload
docs.text:
❌ Upload Failed

Validation failed with the following errors:

🚫 Errors (2)
- Row 23: Missing required fields: 'variant_index'. Expected columns: ...
- Duplicate variant indices found: [15, 42]. Each variant_index must be unique.

Note: If any record has a critical error, the entire upload is rejected to maintain data integrity.

docs.title: Configuring Thresholds
docs.text: Thresholds can be customised in parsing/config.py:

docs.title: Validation Rules
docs.text:
VALIDATION_RULES = {
    # Sequence length bounds
    'sequence_min_length': 100,
    'sequence_max_length': 10000,

    # Generic yield warnings (fallback)
    'yield_min_warning': 50.0,
    'yield_max_warning': 2000.0,

    # Per-metric overrides
    'dna_yield_max_warning': 2000.0,
    'protein_yield_min_warning': 40.0,
    'protein_yield_max_warning': 1000.0,

    # Allowed DNA characters
    'allowed_dna_chars': set('ATGCN'),
}

docs.title: QC Mode Settings
docs.text:
# Enable percentile-based adaptive thresholds
QC_PERCENTILE_MODE = True
QC_PERCENTILE_LOW = 1.0    # Lower percentile (P1)
QC_PERCENTILE_HIGH = 99.0  # Upper percentile (P99)
QC_MIN_SAMPLES_FOR_PERCENTILES = 30

# Critical safety limits (always enforced)
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 300.0
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 5000.0
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 20.0
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 2000.0

docs.title: Error vs Warning Behaviour
docs.text: Condition Behaviour Upload Result Error Record is invalid ❌ Entire upload rejected Warning Record is unusual but valid ⚠️ Upload succeeds, warning logged

docs.title: What Generates Errors
docs.text:
• Missing required fields
  • Invalid data types (non-numeric yields, non-integer variant_index)
  • Invalid DNA characters in sequence
  • Empty sequence
  • Values outside critical safety limits
  • Duplicate variant indices

docs.title: What Generates Warnings
docs.text:
• Negative yield values
  • Extreme yields (outside P1/P99)
  • Unusual sequence length
  • Missing generation 0
  • Gaps in generation numbers
  • Orphaned parent references

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Problem Cause Solution "Missing required fields" Column names don't match expected aliases Check column headers match the accepted aliases "variant_index must be an integer" Non-numeric values in ID column Ensure variant indices are whole numbers "Invalid characters in sequence" Sequence contains characters other than A,T,G,C,N Clean sequence data before upload "Duplicate variant indices" Same ID used for multiple records Assign unique IDs to each variant "Connection timed out" Database server unreachable Check Tailscale/VPN connection

docs.title: File Upload Limits
docs.text: Limit Value Maximum file size 50 MB Allowed extensions .tsv, .csv, .json

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition Variant A specific plasmid construct with a unique DNA sequence Generation The round of directed evolution (0 = wild-type ancestor) Parent Variant The variant from which a mutant was derived DNA Yield Quantification of DNA extracted (femtograms) Protein Yield Quantification of protein expressed (picograms) P1/P99 1st and 99th percentiles used for outlier detection Critical Limit Absolute boundary beyond which values are impossible

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and solutions for the parsing and QC system.

docs.title: Quick Diagnostics

flowchart TD
    P[Problem] --> Q1{Server running?}
    Q1 -->|No| S1[Start Flask app]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Database connected?}
    Q2 -->|No| S2[Check Tailscale/DB]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{File valid?}
    Q3 -->|No| S3[Check file format]

docs.title: Flask Server Won't Start
docs.text:
Error: python: command not found

Solution: Use py instead of python on Windows: 

py -m flask run

Error: No module named flask

Solution: Install dependencies: 

py -m pip install -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Database Connection Timeout
docs.text:
Error: connection to server at '100.x.x.x' failed: Connection timed out

Causes:

  1. Tailscale not connected 

    tailscale status
    If offline, reconnect: 
    tailscale up

  2. Database server offline — Contact your teammate to start their machine

  3. Wrong IP address — Verify the Tailscale IP: 

    tailscale status | findstr teammate-machine

docs.title: Authentication Errors
docs.text:
Error: password authentication failed for user

Solution: Verify credentials in your environment or DATABASE_URL: 

# Check current setting
import os
print(os.environ.get('DATABASE_URL'))

docs.title: File Format Errors
docs.text: Error Cause Solution Unsupported file format Wrong extension Use .tsv or .json Unable to parse file Malformed content Check file encoding (UTF-8) Missing required column Column name mismatch Use standard column names

docs.title: QC Validation Errors
docs.text:
Error: DNA yield below critical minimum (300 fg)

Meaning: Value is below instrument detection limit

Solutions:

  • Check if the measurement was correctly recorded
  • Verify units (fg not pg)
  • If valid, adjust critical limits in config.py

Error: Protein yield above critical maximum (2000 pg)

Meaning: Value indicates saturation or error

Solutions:

  • Check for pipetting errors
  • Dilute sample and re-measure
  • If legitimate high-expresser, adjust limits

docs.title: Duplicate Key Errors
docs.text:
Error: duplicate key value violates unique constraint

Meaning: Trying to insert a record that already exists

Solution: The system uses upsert logic—this should be handled automatically. If you see this error:

  1. Check that db_operations.py has the upsert logic for metrics
  2. Verify the constraint definition in the database

docs.title: QC Warnings
docs.text:
Warning: Value below P1 threshold

Meaning: This value is in the bottom 1% of your dataset

Action:

  • Review the sample—may be a biological outlier or technical error
  • If consistently low, check assay conditions
  • No action needed if sample is known low-performer

Warning: Duplicate plasmid_variant_index detected

Meaning: Same variant appears multiple times in the same generation

Action:

  • If intentional replicates, no action needed
  • If data entry error, correct the source file

docs.title: Missing Tables
docs.text:
Error: relation "experiments" does not exist

Solution: Run the database migrations or create tables: 

from parsing.models import db
db.create_all()

docs.title: Column Mismatch
docs.text:
Error: column "experiment_name" does not exist

Cause: Model doesn't match database schema

Solution: Verify column names in parsing/models.py match your database: 

# Check: is it 'name' or 'experiment_name'?
class Experiment(db.Model):
    name = db.Column(db.String)  # Not experiment_name

docs.title: Slow Uploads
docs.text: Symptom Cause Solution Upload hangs Large file Split into smaller batches Slow QC Many records Normal for >10k records Timeout Network latency Check Tailscale connection

docs.title: Memory Errors
docs.text:
Error: MemoryError on large files

Solution: Process in batches: 

# In routes.py, add batch processing
BATCH_SIZE = 1000
for i in range(0, len(records), BATCH_SIZE):
    batch = records[i:i+BATCH_SIZE]
    process_batch(batch)

docs.title: Test Database Connection
docs.text:
from parsing.db_operations import get_db_connection

conn = get_db_connection()
if conn:
    print("✅ Connected")
    conn.close()
else:
    print("❌ Connection failed")

docs.title: Test QC Thresholds
docs.text:
from parsing.qc import compute_thresholds_from_records

# Create test data
test_records = [
    {"dna_yield_fg": 1000, "protein_yield_pg": 500}
    for _ in range(50)
]

thresholds = compute_thresholds_from_records(test_records)
print(thresholds)

docs.title: Run Test Suite
docs.text: py -m pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
1. Check logs: Flask prints errors to the terminal
  2. Run tests: py -m pytest tests/ -v
  3. Check error details: Look at the full traceback in the terminal
  4. Database state: Query the database directly with psql

docs.title: Processing Flow
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant R as Routes
    participant P as Parser
    participant Q as QC
    participant D as Database

    C->>R: POST /parsing/upload
    R->>R: Validate file extension

    alt Invalid Extension
        R-->>C: Error: Unsupported format
    end

    R->>P: Parse file
    P-->>R: Parsed records

    R->>Q: Run QC checks
    Q->>Q: Compute thresholds
    Q->>Q: Validate records
    Q-->>R: QC report

    else Warnings or OK

        D-->>R: Success

    end

docs.title: Insert Flow

docs.text:
Tab-separated values with a header row.

plasmid_variant_index   generation  dna_yield_fg    protein_yield_pg

BSU_Pol_002 G0  1678.90 512.34

docs.text: Column Type Description plasmid_variant_index String Unique variant identifier generation String Generation label (G0, G1, etc.) dna_yield_fg Float DNA yield in femtograms protein_yield_pg Float Protein yield in picograms

docs.text:
Any additional columns are preserved as extra metadata:

BSU_Pol_001 G0  1523.45 456.78  Wild type   A1

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
Encoding

Files must be UTF-8 encoded. Excel exports may use Windows-1252 by default—save as "CSV UTF-8" or "Unicode Text".

TSV vs CSV

The parser expects tabs as separators. If using CSV (commas), save as TSV first or modify the delimiter.

Empty Rows

Trailing empty rows are ignored. Rows with partial data generate errors.

docs.title: From Excel
docs.text:
1. Open your data in Excel
  2. Ensure columns match required names
  3. File → Save As → Select "Text (Tab delimited) (*.txt)"
  4. Rename .txt to .tsv

docs.title: From Python
docs.text:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'plasmid_variant_index': ['BSU_001', 'BSU_002'],
    'generation': ['G0', 'G0'],
    'dna_yield_fg': [1523.45, 1678.90],
    'protein_yield_pg': [456.78, 512.34]
})

# TSV export
df.to_csv('output.tsv', sep='\t', index=False)

# JSON export
df.to_json('output.json', orient='records', indent=2)

docs.title: Quality Control Overview
docs.text: The QC system uses a two-tier threshold architecture to identify data quality issues while accommodating batch-to-batch variation.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["📥 Input Data"]
        R[Parsed Records]
    end

    subgraph Adaptive["🎯 Tier 1: Adaptive Thresholds"]
        direction TB
        A1[Collect all yield values]
        A2[Calculate P1 & P99 percentiles]
        A3[Set warning bounds]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph Critical["🚨 Tier 2: Critical Limits"]
        direction TB
        C1[Fixed biological limits]
        C2[Instrument specifications]
        C1 --- C2
    end

    subgraph Validation["✅ Validation"]
        V1[Per-Record Checks]
        V2[Cross-Record Checks]
    end

    subgraph Output["📤 Results"]
        O1[⚠️ Warnings]
        O2[❌ Errors]
    end

    R --> Adaptive
    R --> Critical
    Adaptive --> V1
    Critical --> V1
    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> O1
    V2 --> O2

docs.title: Tier 1: Adaptive Warnings (Percentile-Based)
docs.text:
Warnings flag statistical outliers relative to the current dataset.

%%{init: {'theme': 'base', 'themeVariables': { 'fontSize': '14px'}}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph Dataset["Your Dataset (n=301)"]
        D1["DNA Yields: 389 - 2000 fg"]
    end

    subgraph Calculation["Percentile Calculation"]
        P1["P1 = 395 fg"]
        P99["P99 = 1850 fg"]
    end

    subgraph Result["Warning Thresholds"]
        W1["< 395 fg → ⚠️ Low"]
        W2["> 1850 fg → ⚠️ High"]
    end

    Dataset --> Calculation --> Result

Why percentiles?

Method Problem Fixed thresholds Don't adapt to batch variation Mean ± 2σ (Z-scores) Assumes normal distribution P1/P99 Percentiles ✅ Non-parametric, robust to skew

docs.title: Tier 2: Critical Errors (Fixed Limits)
docs.text:
Errors flag values that are biologically impossible or indicate instrument failure.

Metric Critical Min Critical Max Rationale DNA Yield 300 fg 5,000 fg Below detection / saturation Protein Yield 20 pg 2,000 pg Expression system limits

Critical Limit Violations

Values outside critical limits always generate errors and the upload is rejected.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Thresholds are configured in parsing/config.py:

Adaptive SettingsCritical Limits 
# Percentile-based thresholds
QC_PERCENTILE_MODE = True
QC_PERCENTILE_LOW = 1.0      # P1
QC_PERCENTILE_HIGH = 99.0    # P99
QC_MIN_SAMPLES_FOR_PERCENTILES = 30

# Absolute safety limits
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 300.0
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 5000.0
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 20.0
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 2000.0

docs.title: Decision Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    V[Yield Value] --> C1{Below Critical Min?}
    C1 -->|Yes| E1[❌ ERROR: Reject]
    C1 -->|No| C2{Above Critical Max?}
    C2 -->|Yes| E2[❌ ERROR: Reject]
    C2 -->|No| W1{Below P1?}
    W1 -->|Yes| W3[⚠️ WARNING: Flag]
    W1 -->|No| W2{Above P99?}
    W2 -->|Yes| W4[⚠️ WARNING: Flag]
    W2 -->|No| OK[✅ OK: Accept]

    W3 --> OK2[Record Accepted]
    W4 --> OK2

    style E1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style W3 fill:#ffd43b
    style W4 fill:#ffd43b
    style OK fill:#51cf66
    style OK2 fill:#51cf66

docs.title: Benefits of This Approach
docs.text:
Adaptive to Each Experiment

Different batches, reagent lots, and assay conditions produce different expected ranges. Percentile-based thresholds automatically adjust.

Catches Real Errors

Critical limits based on physical reality ensure impossible values are always flagged.

Reduces False Positives

Using P1/P99 (not P5/P95) means only the most extreme 2% of values are flagged, reducing noise.

Non-Parametric

Works correctly even when yield distributions are skewed (common in biological data).

docs.title: Threshold Configuration
docs.text: This page details how to configure and customise QC thresholds for your specific experimental conditions.

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text:
All threshold settings are in parsing/config.py:

parsing/config.py
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# QC Threshold Configuration
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Adaptive (Percentile-Based) Thresholds
QC_PERCENTILE_MODE = True
QC_PERCENTILE_LOW = 1.0       # Bottom percentile (P1)
QC_PERCENTILE_HIGH = 99.0     # Top percentile (P99)
QC_MIN_SAMPLES_FOR_PERCENTILES = 30

# Critical Limits - DNA Yield (femtograms)
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 300.0
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 5000.0

# Critical Limits - Protein Yield (picograms)
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 20.0
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 2000.0

docs.title: Percentile Calculation
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Input"]
        D["All yield values<br/>(n samples)"]
    end

    subgraph Check["Sample Check"]
        C{n ≥ 30?}
    end

    subgraph Calculate["Calculate"]
        P1["P1 = numpy.percentile(values, 1)"]
        P99["P99 = numpy.percentile(values, 99)"]
    end

    subgraph Fallback["Fallback"]
        F["Use critical limits only"]
    end

    D --> C
    C -->|Yes| Calculate
    C -->|No| Fallback

    style C fill:#4dabf7
    style P1 fill:#51cf66
    style P99 fill:#51cf66
    style F fill:#ffd43b

docs.title: Why 30 Samples Minimum?
docs.text: With fewer than 30 data points, percentile estimates become unreliable. The system falls back to critical limits only.

docs.title: Adjusting Percentile Sensitivity
docs.text:
Setting Effect Use Case P1/P99 (default) Flags 2% of values Standard screening P5/P95 Flags 10% of values Strict QC P0.1/P99.9 Flags 0.2% of values Relaxed QC

To change:

# Stricter thresholds (more warnings)
QC_PERCENTILE_LOW = 5.0
QC_PERCENTILE_HIGH = 95.0

# Looser thresholds (fewer warnings)
QC_PERCENTILE_LOW = 0.1
QC_PERCENTILE_HIGH = 99.9

docs.title: DNA Yield Limits
docs.text:
graph LR
    subgraph Limits["DNA Yield Critical Limits"]
        MIN["300 fg<br/>Detection Limit"]
        MAX["5000 fg<br/>Saturation"]
    end

    subgraph Rationale["Based On"]
        R1["qPCR sensitivity"]
        R2["Fluorimeter range"]
        R3["Historical data"]
    end

    R1 --> MIN
    R2 --> MAX
    R3 --> MIN
    R3 --> MAX
Boundary Value Rationale Minimum 300 fg Below typical qPCR detection limit Maximum 5,000 fg Above indicates contamination or pipetting error

docs.title: Protein Yield Limits
docs.text: Boundary Value Rationale Minimum 20 pg Below BCA assay sensitivity Maximum 2,000 pg Expression system saturation

docs.title: High-Throughput Screening
docs.text:
For 384-well assays with lower volumes:

# Adjusted for microplate format
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 150.0   # Lower detection
DNA_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 3000.0  # Lower saturation

docs.title: Cell-Free Expression
docs.text:
For CFPS systems with higher yields:

PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MIN = 50.0
PROTEIN_YIELD_CRITICAL_MAX = 5000.0

docs.title: Threshold Interaction Diagram
docs.text:
%%{init: {'theme': 'base'}}%%
graph TB
    subgraph Critical["Critical Limits (Fixed)"]
        CL["300 - 5000 fg"]
    end

    subgraph Adaptive["Adaptive Thresholds (Per-Batch)"]
        AT["P1 - P99"]
    end

    subgraph Overlap["Overlap Zone"]
        direction LR
        O1["CRITICAL MIN"]
        O2["P1"]
        O3["P99"]
        O4["CRITICAL MAX"]
        O1 -.-> O2 -.-> O3 -.-> O4
    end

    Critical --> Overlap
    Adaptive --> Overlap

    subgraph Outcomes["Outcomes"]
        E1["❌ Below 300: ERROR"]
        W1["⚠️ 300-P1: WARNING"]
        OK["✅ P1-P99: OK"]
        W2["⚠️ P99-5000: WARNING"]
        E2["❌ Above 5000: ERROR"]
    end

    Overlap --> Outcomes

    style E1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style W1 fill:#ffd43b
    style W2 fill:#ffd43b
    style OK fill:#51cf66

docs.title: Special Cases
docs.text:
Adaptive Thresholds Inside Critical Limits

If your P1 calculates to 250 fg (below the 300 fg critical minimum), the critical limit takes precedence. Values below 300 fg are always errors.

Small Batches

For batches with fewer than 30 samples, only critical limits apply. Consider pooling related experiments for threshold calculation.

docs.title: Testing Your Configuration
docs.text:
After modifying thresholds, run the test suite:

py -m pytest tests/test_qc.py -v

Check the threshold computation:

from parsing.qc import compute_thresholds_from_records

# Your parsed records
thresholds = compute_thresholds_from_records(records)
print(f"DNA P1: {thresholds['dna_yield_low']}")
print(f"DNA P99: {thresholds['dna_yield_high']}")

docs.title: Check Categories

        direction TB
        subgraph PerRecord["Per-Record Checks"]
            PR1[Required Fields]
            PR2[Yield Range]

docs.title: Per-Record Checks
docs.text: These checks validate each row individually.

docs.title: 1. Required Fields
docs.text:
Field Required Example plasmid_variant_index ✅ Yes BSU_Pol_001 generation ✅ Yes G0, G1, G2 dna_yield_fg ✅ Yes 1523.45 protein_yield_pg ✅ Yes 456.78

Missing Field Error

ERROR: Row 45 - Missing required field: dna_yield_fg

docs.title: DNA Yield Checks
docs.text: Check Type Condition Result Critical Low < 300 fg ❌ ERROR Warning Low < P1 ⚠️ WARNING Normal P1 ≤ x ≤ P99 ✅ OK Warning High > P99 ⚠️ WARNING Critical High > 5000 fg ❌ ERROR

docs.title: Protein Yield Checks
docs.text: Check Type Condition Result Critical Low < 20 pg ❌ ERROR Warning Low < P1 ⚠️ WARNING Normal P1 ≤ x ≤ P99 ✅ OK Warning High > P99 ⚠️ WARNING Critical High > 2000 pg ❌ ERROR

docs.title: 3. Data Type Validation
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    V[Value] --> T{Type Check}
    T -->|Valid Number| OK[✅ Accept]
    T -->|Empty/Null| M[Handle Missing]
    T -->|Invalid| E[❌ ERROR]

    M --> M1{Required?}
    M1 -->|Yes| E
    M1 -->|No| OK

    style OK fill:#51cf66
    style E fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
Field Expected Type Invalid Examples dna_yield_fg Float "N/A", "pending", "" protein_yield_pg Float "null", "#REF!", "-" generation String (must not be empty) plasmid_variant_index String (must not be empty)

docs.title: Cross-Record Checks
docs.text: These checks validate relationships between records.

docs.title: 1. Duplicate Detection
docs.text:
Checks for duplicate plasmid_variant_index within the same generation.

flowchart LR
    subgraph Records["Uploaded Records"]
        R1["BSU_Pol_001 (G1)"]
        R2["BSU_Pol_002 (G1)"]
        R3["BSU_Pol_001 (G1)"]
    end

    subgraph Check["Duplicate Check"]
        D{Same variant<br/>+ Same gen?}
    end

    subgraph Result["Result"]
        W["⚠️ WARNING:<br/>Duplicate detected"]
    end

    R1 --> D
    R3 --> D
    D -->|Yes| W

    style W fill:#ffd43b

Duplicate Warning

Validates that batch metadata is consistent across files.

Check Description Experiment Name Must match existing experiment or will be created Generation Sequence G0 → G1 → G2 (no gaps) Wild Type Reference Must exist in database

flowchart LR
    subgraph Valid["✅ Valid Sequences"]
        V1["G0"]
        V2["G0 → G1"]
        V3["G0 → G1 → G2"]
    end

    subgraph Invalid["❌ Invalid"]

docs.title: Check Execution Order
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as File
    participant P as Parser
    participant V as Validator
    participant DB as Database

    F->>P: Upload file
    P->>P: Parse records

    loop Each Record
        P->>V: Per-record validation
        V-->>P: Errors/Warnings
    end

    P->>V: Cross-record validation
    V-->>P: Errors/Warnings

    alt No Errors
        P->>DB: Insert records
        DB-->>P: Success
    else Has Errors
        P-->>F: Reject with details
    end

docs.title: Error vs Warning Behaviour
docs.text:
Severity Effect Record Saved? ❌ ERROR Upload rejected No ⚠️ WARNING Flagged but accepted Yes

One Error = Full Rejection

If any record has an error, the entire upload is rejected. Fix all errors before re-uploading.

docs.title: Customising Checks
docs.text:
To add custom validation rules, modify parsing/qc.py:

def custom_check(record: dict) -> list[dict]:
    """Add your custom validation logic."""
    warnings = []

    # Example: Flag specific variant patterns
    if "control" in record.get("plasmid_variant_index", "").lower():
        if record.get("dna_yield_fg", 0) < 500:
            warnings.append({
                "field": "dna_yield_fg",
                "message": "Control variant has unusually low yield"
            })

    return warnings

docs.title: Activity Score Calculations
docs.text: This page explains the unified activity metric used throughout the reporting and visualisation pipeline. The goal is to reduce two raw measurements into one comparable score that reflects functional output relative to expression.

docs.title: Core formula
docs.text:
The final derived metric is:

  • activity_score = dna_yield_norm / protein_yield_norm

The normalized inputs depend on which baseline strategy is available for the experiment.

docs.title: Preferred strategy: WT normalization
docs.text:
When valid wild-type (WT) controls exist for every generation, the pipeline normalizes each variant against the WT of the same generation:

  • dna_yield_norm = dna_yield_raw / dna_wt
  • protein_yield_norm = protein_yield_raw / protein_wt

This is the preferred route because it removes generation-specific assay variation while keeping the comparison tied to a real biological control.

docs.title: Fallback strategy: generation-median normalization
docs.text:
Real datasets may have missing or incomplete WT controls. In that case, the report pipeline falls back to a WT-free comparison:

  • dna_yield_norm = dna_yield_raw / median_dna_yield_for_generation
  • protein_yield_norm = protein_yield_raw / median_protein_yield_for_generation

The same final ratio is then applied:

  • activity_score = dna_yield_norm / protein_yield_norm

This keeps activity scores computable and comparable within the experiment even when the WT control data is incomplete.

docs.title: Code paths used in this project
docs.text:
• WT-based normalization and QC:
  • app/services/analysis/activity_score.py
  • compute_stage4_metrics(...)
  • Fallback median normalization:
  • scripts/run_report.py
  • compute_activity_score_fallback(...)
  • Scoring mode selection (auto, wt, fallback):
  • scripts/run_report.py

docs.title: Worked example
docs.text:
Variant measurements:

  • dna_yield_raw = 120
  • protein_yield_raw = 40

docs.title: Case 1: WT normalization available
docs.text:
WT baseline for the same generation:

  • dna_wt = 100
  • protein_wt = 50

Then:

  • dna_yield_norm = 120 / 100 = 1.20
  • protein_yield_norm = 40 / 50 = 0.80
  • activity_score = 1.20 / 0.80 = 1.50

Interpretation: the variant shows roughly 50% stronger functional output relative to its expression than the WT baseline.

docs.title: Case 2: generation-median fallback
docs.text:
If WT controls are unavailable, assume the generation medians are:

  • dna_median = 90
  • protein_median = 45

Then:

  • dna_yield_norm = 120 / 90 = 1.33
  • protein_yield_norm = 40 / 45 = 0.89
  • activity_score = 1.33 / 0.89 ≈ 1.49

This produces a comparable estimate while remaining robust to missing control rows.

docs.title: Interpretation guide
docs.text:
• activity_score > 1: improved functional efficiency relative to the chosen baseline
  • activity_score ≈ 1: performance near the normalization baseline
  • activity_score < 1: reduced efficiency relative to the baseline

These values should always be interpreted within the same experiment context, because the normalization strategy is applied per generation.

docs.title: What gets stored
docs.text:
When a variant passes metric QC, the pipeline writes:

  • dna_yield_norm
  • protein_yield_norm
  • activity_score

All three are stored as ratio-style outputs and then reused by:

  • Top 10 ranking across the experiment
  • activity score distribution plots
  • downstream report summaries and bonus analyses

docs.title: Database overview
docs.text: This database stores proteins, variants, mutations, metrics, and lineage relationships. It also includes extra tables and materialized views used to power the bonus visualizations.

docs.title: Core tables
docs.text:
• users, wild_type_proteins, protein_features
  • experiments, generations, variants, mutations
  • metrics, experiment_metadata

docs.title: Entity flow
docs.text:
The main directional path for analysis is: experiments -> generations -> variants -> metrics/mutations

This is the path most report and plot queries follow.

docs.title: Key relationships
docs.text:
• experiments -> generations -> variants
  • variants -> mutations (DNA and protein)
  • metrics records per-variant or WT control values
  • protein_features defines domain ranges for enrichment

docs.title: Metrics model (important)
docs.text:
• Raw metrics are stored with metric_type='raw'
  • Normalized metrics use metric_type='normalized'
  • Final activity scoring uses metric_type='derived' and metric_name='activity_score'

At insert time, trigger logic derives generation_id for metrics rows from either: - variant_id, or - wt_control_id

docs.title: Lineage and analysis tables
docs.text:
• variant_lineage_closure
  • variant_sequence_analysis

docs.title: Bonus visualization tables
docs.text:
• variant_performance_rankings: top variants per generation
  • mutation_introduction_events: first-generation mutation appearances
  • embedding_runs and embedding_points: 2D coordinates for landscapes

docs.title: Materialized view keys
docs.text:
• mv_activity_landscape is unique by (embedding_run_id, variant_id)
  • mv_domain_mutation_enrichment is unique by (generation_id, wt_id, feature_type, start_position, end_position)

docs.title: Materialized views
docs.text:
• mv_activity_landscape: denormalized 3D landscape data
  • mv_domain_mutation_enrichment: mutation density by protein domain

docs.title: Database setup (Tailscale)
docs.text: This page is an operational checklist for exposing a local PostgreSQL server to teammates over Tailscale.

docs.title: Install and start PostgreSQL
docs.text:
brew install postgresql
brew services start postgresql@18
psql --version

docs.title: Create local DB user and database
docs.text:
createuser <DB_ADMIN_USER> --pwprompt
psql -d postgres -c "ALTER ROLE <DB_ADMIN_USER> CREATEDB;"
createdb -O <DB_ADMIN_USER> bio727p_group_project
psql -U <DB_ADMIN_USER> -d bio727p_group_project -h localhost

docs.title: Install and start Tailscale
docs.text:
brew install --formula tailscale
sudo brew services start tailscale
sudo tailscale up
tailscale status
tailscale ip -4

Save the IPv4 address; teammates will connect using this host.

docs.title: Configure PostgreSQL to listen externally
docs.text:
Find config paths:

psql -d postgres -c "SHOW config_file;"
psql -d postgres -c "SHOW hba_file;"

Edit postgresql.conf:

listen_addresses = '*'

Edit pg_hba.conf and add:

# Local
local   all             all                                     trust
host    all             all             127.0.0.1/32            scram-sha-256
host    all             all             ::1/128                 scram-sha-256

# Tailscale network
host    all             all             100.64.0.0/10           scram-sha-256

Restart and verify:

brew services restart postgresql@18
psql -d postgres -c "SHOW listen_addresses;"
lsof -nP -iTCP:5432 -sTCP:LISTEN

docs.title: Set password for host user
docs.text: ALTER ROLE <DB_ADMIN_USER> WITH PASSWORD '<PASSWORD>';

docs.title: Create team role and grant privileges
docs.text:
DO $$
BEGIN
  IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = 'bio727p_role') THEN
    CREATE ROLE bio727p_role NOLOGIN;
  END IF;
END $$;

GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE bio727p_group_project TO bio727p_role;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO bio727p_role;
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO bio727p_role;
GRANT USAGE, SELECT, UPDATE ON ALL SEQUENCES IN SCHEMA public TO bio727p_role;

Set default privileges for future objects:

ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES FOR ROLE <DB_ADMIN_USER> IN SCHEMA public
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON TABLES TO bio727p_role;

ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES FOR ROLE <DB_ADMIN_USER> IN SCHEMA public
GRANT USAGE, SELECT, UPDATE ON SEQUENCES TO bio727p_role;

docs.title: Create individual teammate users
docs.text:
CREATE USER <TEAMMATE_USER> WITH PASSWORD '<TEAMMATE_PASSWORD>';
GRANT bio727p_role TO <TEAMMATE_USER>;
ALTER ROLE <TEAMMATE_USER> LOGIN;

docs.title: Connection test
docs.text: psql "postgresql://<TEAMMATE_USER>:<TEAMMATE_PASSWORD>@<TAILSCALE_IP>:5432/bio727p_group_project"

If teammates need read access to the bonus materialized views:

GRANT SELECT ON MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_activity_landscape TO bio727p_role;
GRANT SELECT ON MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_domain_mutation_enrichment TO bio727p_role;

If refresh fails for non-owners, run refresh as the DB owner account or convert the function to SECURITY DEFINER with a safe search_path.

docs.title: Metric QC & Validation
docs.text: This page summarizes how activity-score rows are gated and how to verify that stored outputs are scientifically plausible before you trust the rankings and plots built from them.

docs.title: QC exclusions in WT-based scoring
docs.text:
WT-based activity-score rows are only written for variants that pass the stage-4 checks in compute_stage4_metrics(...).

Typical exclusion reasons include:

  • missing DNA or protein raw values
  • non-positive raw values
  • missing WT baseline values
  • invalid WT baseline values
  • normalized protein values that are too small for stable division

Only rows that pass these checks are persisted as normalized and derived metrics.

docs.title: QC exclusions in fallback scoring
docs.text:
When the report pipeline uses generation-median normalization instead of WT controls, rows can still be rejected if:

  • the variant lacks required identifiers
  • raw inputs cannot be converted to numeric values
  • generation medians are undefined or zero
  • the resulting normalized or derived values are non-finite

Those rows are marked as invalid fallback rows and excluded from derived output inserts.

docs.title: What gets written when QC passes
docs.text:
For each accepted variant, the pipeline writes:

  • dna_yield_norm
  • protein_yield_norm
  • activity_score

These outputs become the basis for:

  • Top 10 ranking
  • activity distribution views
  • downstream visual summaries

docs.title: Practical validation checks
docs.text:
When validating metric outputs, check:

  1. that raw DNA and protein inputs exist
  2. that WT baselines exist for the same generation, or that fallback scoring was intentionally used
  3. that activity_score rows were actually written
  4. that per-generation counts look plausible
  5. that the top-ranked rows in the report match the highest stored activity scores

docs.title: Minimal SQL checks
docs.text:
select count(*) as n_activity_scores
from metrics
where metric_name = 'activity_score'
  and metric_type = 'derived';

select metric_name, metric_type, count(*) as n
from metrics
group by metric_name, metric_type
order by metric_name, metric_type;

select
  g.experiment_id,
  count(*) as n_activity_scores
from metrics m
join variants v on v.variant_id = m.variant_id
join generations g on g.generation_id = v.generation_id
where m.metric_name = 'activity_score'
  and m.metric_type = 'derived'
group by g.experiment_id
order by g.experiment_id;

docs.title: What to investigate if results look wrong
docs.text:
• no activity_score rows:
  • raw metrics may be missing
  • WT baselines may be missing or invalid
  • fallback normalization may have produced invalid medians
  • unusually low row counts:
  • too many rows may be failing QC
  • unexpected score ranges:
  • denominator values may be unstable
  • experiment or generation filtering may be incorrect

Use the generated analysis CSVs alongside these checks when comparing stored metrics to the visible plots and rankings.

docs.title: Metric Storage & Types
docs.text: This page focuses on how metrics are structured, categorized, and persisted in the database so that recalculation and reporting remain predictable.

docs.title: Where metrics live
docs.text:
The main metric rows are stored in:

  • metrics

An optional supporting dictionary also exists:

  • metric_definitions

The metric_definitions table provides descriptions, naming conventions, and units, while the metrics table holds the actual experiment data.

docs.title: Metric classes used in practice
docs.text:
The platform writes three main classes of metric rows:

  • raw: direct experimental measurements
  • normalized: baseline-scaled ratios
  • derived: computed values such as activity_score

Examples:

  • dna_yield_raw
  • protein_yield_raw
  • dna_yield_norm
  • protein_yield_norm
  • activity_score

docs.title: Row ownership
docs.text:
Each metric row is attached to either:

  • a variant (variant_id)
  • or a WT control (wt_control_id)

This allows the same metric table to store both experimental variant values and wild-type baseline measurements.

docs.title: Generation linkage
docs.text: generation_id is populated automatically from the owning variant or WT control record. This keeps the metric aligned with the correct generation even when the write path only provides variant_id or wt_control_id.

docs.title: Uniqueness model
docs.text:
For variant-scoped metrics, one logical row is expected for each combination of:

  • generation
  • variant
  • metric name
  • metric type

WT control metrics follow the same idea, keyed by wt_control_id instead of variant_id.

This keeps recalculation idempotent and prevents uncontrolled duplicate rows.

docs.title: Upsert behavior
docs.text:
Metric rows are written through an upsert path, so recalculations update the latest value instead of inserting a second logical copy.

Code path:

  • src/analysis_MPL/metrics.py
  • upsert_variant_metrics(...)

Behavior:

  • insert the metric if it does not exist
  • update value and unit if the metric already exists

docs.title: Units and interpretability
docs.text: For normalized and derived score rows, the expected unit is a ratio-like value. This matters because downstream pages compare those values directly rather than treating them as absolute assay measurements.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
This storage model is what allows the platform to:

  • recompute activity scores safely
  • regenerate reports without duplicating metric rows
  • rank variants using the latest consistent values
  • trace a derived score back to the raw measurements that produced it

docs.title: Metrics Overview
docs.text: The platform uses a unified metric system so that raw assay values, normalized values, and final derived scores all remain traceable to the same experiment and generation context. This is what makes variant ranking, distribution plots, and report generation internally consistent.

docs.title: Metric categories
docs.text:
Three metric classes are used throughout the pipeline:

  • raw: direct assay measurements such as dna_yield_raw and protein_yield_raw
  • normalized: baseline-scaled values such as dna_yield_norm and protein_yield_norm
  • derived: higher-level outputs computed from normalized values, primarily activity_score

docs.title: Unified activity workflow
docs.text:
The high-level metric flow is:

  1. store raw DNA and protein yields
  2. attempt wild-type (WT) baseline normalization for the same generation
  3. if WT baselines are unavailable, fall back to generation-median normalization
  4. compute the final activity_score
  5. persist normalized and derived rows for ranking, plotting, and reporting

This keeps the activity score computable even when real experimental datasets have missing WT control measurements.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
Because all downstream views read from the same metric layer, the metric system acts as the common language between:

  • sequence-aware reporting
  • Top 10 ranking
  • activity score distributions
  • lineage summaries
  • protein similarity and bonus views

docs.title: What this section covers
docs.text:
Use the pages in this group for:

  • Metrics Overview: the shared purpose of the metric layer
  • Metric Storage & Types: how rows are structured and upserted
  • Activity Score Calculations: the formula, fallback behavior, and worked examples
  • Metric QC & Validation: which rows are excluded and how to check outputs
